explanation: FALSE POSITIVE. The CEI violation is structurally present — fivePillarsToken.transferFrom() and burnFrom() are called before updating investor.lastDepositTimestamp and investor.totalDeposit. However, the vulnerability is not exploitable by an unprivileged external attacker because: (1) fivePillarsToken is the protocol's own immutable trusted token set at deploy time with no setter, not attacker-controlled; (2) standard ERC-20 transferFrom has no callback mechanism — it cannot call back to the sender (investorAddress/attacker) during transfer; (3) the token interface shows only standard ERC-20 methods (transferFrom, burnFrom, mint, balanceOf, approve) with no evidence of ERC-777 tokensToSend hooks, ERC-677 transferAndCall, or any other callback mechanism that would enable reentrancy; (4) the attack scenario explicitly states "Assume a malicious FivePillarsToken contract" — this requires compromising the trusted protocol token (a privileged/owner-level conspiracy), not an external permissionless attack. Without a callback hook in the token, control never returns to an attacker during transferFrom, making the depositDelay bypass and totalDeposit inflation impossible via this vector. The finding conflates a code-quality CEI violation with an exploitable high-severity reentrancy vulnerability.
